speaker: interviewer
text: April 24, 1996. Survivor, Sonja Waitzner-- S-O-N-J-A W-A-I-T-Z-N-E-R. Her maiden name is Friedman-- F-R-I-E-D-M-A-N. The interviewer, Elisabeth Pozzi-Thanner-- E-L-I-S-A-B-E-T-H P-O-Z-Z-I T-H-A-N-N-E-R. We're in New York City, in the United States of America. Language is going to be English. Today is April 24, 1996. The survivor is Sonja Waitzner-- S-O-N-J-A, W-A-I-T-Z-N-E-R. Her maiden name is Friedman-- F-R-I-E-D-M-A-N. Interviewer, Elisabeth Pozzi-Thanner-- E-L-I-S-A-B-E-T-H P-O-Z-Z-I T-H-A-N-N-E-R. We are in New York City, in the United States of America, and the interview is going to be in English. Would you please tell me your name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, my name is Sonja Waitzner-- S-O-N-J-A Waitzner, W-A-I-T-Z-N-E-R, nee Friedman. I was born December 7, 1926, in Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Friedman for me, please? F-R-I-E-D-M-A-N. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family consisted of my parents, two older brothers, and myself. My oldest brother's name is Leon, the middle one is Norbert, and I'm the youngest. In age, we're two years apart each.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Leon, that spells--

speaker: interviewee
text: L-E-O-N, and Norbert is spelled N-O-R-B-E-R-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was Leon born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon was born in 1922, Norbert was born in 1924, and I was born in 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother Leon was born in Poland--

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and Norbert in Vienna. But Leon came as a baby to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Poland was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon was born in-- I forgot the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Skalat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Skalat, Poland. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you-- would you spell that for me please?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-K-A-L-A-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents, who were your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, my father and mother were both from the same town, Skalat, Poland. My father came to Vienna as a youngster. He returned to Poland to marry my mother, and subsequently my brother was born there. Then he came back to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim Friedman.

speaker: interviewer
text: I need the spelling again, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-A-I-M Friedman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sali, S-A-L-I, Friedman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goldstein, G-O-L-D-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: So both of them came from Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Originally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Originally.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Vienna in a mixed area, mixed neighborhood of Jews and non-Jews. Our street was almost exclusively non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the street you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Staudinger Gasse in the 20th

speaker: interviewer
text: District.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bezirk. Well, what would you call? 20th district, I would call that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I need to ask you for the spelling of Staudinger Gasse.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. S-T-A-U-D-I-N-G-E-R G-A-S-S-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a merchant, and he was gone during the day. And my mother was not quite well, as far back as I can remember. And we had good times, and we had very bad times at home. It turned out that my mother was mentally ill, and it affected all our lives. I remember that when my father left in the morning, I didn't want him to leave. I held onto his legs. And when he came home at night, it was like a holiday. But we had-- we played outside, was a safe street. And I remember happy times. I remember that on weekends, like on a Sunday, when the weather permitting, we went to Schonbrunn. We went to Prater, which is the equivalent of Coney Island. And we went to a mountain close to Vienna. And we went to the circus, and we went swimming. Those were happy times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who brought you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our father and our mother, together. But later on, I remember that our father took us. We children played a lot outside, all children played on the street. It was perfectly safe. I remember Norbert had just one Jewish friend, because there were no Jewish children on our street. I played with the girls, my brother Leon played soccer all the time. But anti-Semitism was rampant in Austria. And if somebody didn't like something, as small as the kid was, they would say to my brother, saujuden, which means pig Jew. Or they would have expressions that were derogatory and insulting to the Jews. And I will say it in German and translate it, because in German, it rhymed. Jud, Jud, spuck in hut. Sag der Vater, das ist gud. Translated-- Jew, Jew, spit in your hat. And tell your father, this is good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was brought up, really from the beginning, that somehow that we are different. And life was hard in that respect. Yet I was a happy kid, I played outside. Whatever was in-- in-- in style at that time. Diabolo, it was two sticks with a string throwing into the air, it was real fun. And my brother always played soccer. Leon always played soccer, and Norbert played with his friend or with me. Did you have any relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one uncle. My father had one brother living in Vienna, and they were not particularly close. He was really [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] half a stranger to us. They had two children, and even we didn't get along well. And those were the only relatives we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Max Friedman, and the daughter was Gerti Friedman.

speaker: interviewer
text: That spells?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gerti is G-E-R-T-I. They live in Canada. The children, anyway. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When in 1934, my mother became so bad that she had to be institutionalized. And that was really very traumatic. I had just turned 8, Norbert 10, and Leon 12. My father was there alone with three young children, and he had to do something drastic. He found a children's home for Leon and myself, and kept the middle son, Norbert, with him to keep one child with him. And he sent us to a children's home called [NON-ENGLISH] That children's home was founded by Fritz Kreisler, the violinist and composer. And it was administered by his sister, Ella Kreisler. There were almost no Jewish children there, it was all Christian children. To me, it was like being sent away to China. It seemed-- I understand it was only two or three hours away from Vienna, but to me it was a world away. And the bad part was that visiting time was only once a month, for a few hours, on the Sunday every fourth Sunday. And that was the happiest day of the month. A few days before, I became excited, and it was just great. When my father came, everybody said, Sonja, your father is coming, your father's coming. He was the most popular man. He brought the most of goodies for Leon and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of man was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a beautiful man. Handsome, he looks always distinguished, even as a young man. And he was loving, and he lived for us children. I adored him from day one until he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he like to do when he was not working?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he was not working, he devoted his time to us. And he belonged to Jewish war veterans in Vienna. My father had served under-- in the First World War in the Austrian army. And that gave him the opportunity to become an Austrian citizen. Otherwise, we would have never been citizens. He had that in mind, that when he gets married and has children, he wants everybody to be Austrian, so we should be all equal in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a religious household?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very religious. They both came from religious house. I remember my mother making challah Friday morning. She was braiding the challah. I remember her buying carp, fresh carp, and the carp was lying on the table. And it started to jump, and I got scared. It was still-- The nerves were still jumping. And she cooked, and she baked. I mean, when she was well-- before she got really sick, she was never quite well-- she was really terrific to us. I remember, before Passover, she used to take sour cherries and put them in big glass jars with sugar and water, and she made wine for Passover. And I remember one Passover, it was so beautiful. These beautiful dishes. And the little glass-- wine glasses for us children. And my father was praying, reading from the seder. And I remember getting drowsy, and we went to sleep, and he continued. And it was fairly festive and very beautiful. I remember going to first grade, Torstrasse. And, before modesty, learning a poem, and making violet flowers out of crepe paper. And bringing it home, and reciting the poem to my mother. And I remember her hugging me and kissing me. Then I went to second grade, in Vienna, and that was the end of 1934, when my mother was taken into an institution.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been aware of the fact that she was sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was-- I was aware. I was aware that she was not like everybody else. Yes, I-- I was. But my brother and I adjusted quite well in the children's home. It was way out in the country, there was pastures over there. Over the-- over the fence, I saw cows. If I didn't like what we had for lunch, I took the bread and threw it over the fence for the cow. [LAUGHS] When we ate-- when we ate in the garden in the summer. We made excursion. In the summer, at sundown, we were already up in the mountains sometimes. Or we had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went swimming. I even got some piano lessons at one time. It was out in the-- in the summer, we were out in the open. And I remember that one day, we had a rest period after lunch. Everybody was lying down in the grass under the trees, and were given books. And I opened the book, and I started to read, and I fell in love with reading. It was so great, and I did not stop reading ever since. I really loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I ask you, that must have been quite a difference also in the way how you lived? Did you have your own room back home in Vienna, or did you stay with your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your bedroom look like in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in we--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, actually, I stayed still with my parents in the room. And my brothers were in the other room. But in the children's home, we were many children in a huge room. And what I really missed was somebody to cover me, and to cuddle me. And when I saw shadows on the walls, it was sort of scary. There was nobody to reassure you. You were really left on your own. But I felt secure, because my brother was there. Also, he was really separated from the girls. But I knew he was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you see him every once in a while?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once in a while, I saw him. But we were 4 and 1/2 years different of age. His interests were different, but I felt secure that he was there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you ever travel back to Vienna when there were holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was there for 3 and 1/2 years in 1938, when the annexation of Austria occurred. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I saw-- I saw a tremendous change, even in the children's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a hysteria going on among the children, among the adults. The leaflets were flying all over the Austria from the-- from-- from Germany about Hitler. There was such excitement. And I knew that I was different. I really didn't want to be different. I wanted to be one of the-- of everybody. But it wasn't so. And so, in a short period-- oh, I forgot to say that my brother was there for two years, up to the age of 14. And he went back to Vienna because that was the cut off age for children. I was by myself then. But I was-- I was all right by myself, too. Except when the annexation occurred. Well, I was told that I'm going to leave the children's home. All the Jewish children. We were maybe three or four, altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you told?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were told that we have to go to Vienna. And I was very happy. I wanted to go home, anyway. But, of course, I could not go home. So I went to an orphanage in Vienna, a Jewish orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who picked you up? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been my father, unless it was-- we were put on a train, and we were met by my-- I was met by my father. I really don't remember that. But I went to the Jewish girl's orphanage in Vienna. And I really liked it a lot better. I really didn't like to be-- live in the country. I'm really a city person at heart. I've always been.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I was then about 11. In 1938, I was 11-- a little over 11. We were not allowed to go to school with the others. We were not allowed to travel, to use transportation. We were not lot allowed to sit in parks, not on benches, nothing. So we walked from the orphanage, we walked to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: In effect, at that time, I could say that my schooling really ended. Because I could not learn anymore. The life-- our life was so disrupted that my concentration actually failed. And now we took some English lessons, and so forth. But there was so much pressure on-- on the Jewish people, and certainly we children felt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a special friend at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just come back to Vienna, and I made friends in the orphanage. Immediately I made friends. But we were all in the same boat of total oppression. Then, in July-- July 11, 1938, my father received from the courthouse in Vienna a notice of-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what-- what do you call it? Notice to have to leave the apartment. Eviction, notice of eviction. It was dated July 11, 1938. I don't know when it reached my father. But it said there, in effect, that by July 31st, 1938, at 12 o'clock noon, the apartment has to be vacated. The reason is that the Aryans do not wish to live with the non-Aryans. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That was the first big-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, I don't-- I don't know what to call it. It was quite terrible for my father, because he lived together with my two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was told about it. I-- I-- from the children's home in Vienna, I was together with my father and my brothers all the time. By the way, at that point, my father was already getting a divorce. Because the diagnosis-- prognosis and diagnosis for my mother was that she was irrevocably mentally ill, and she will never get better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never saw my mother again. My father took my brothers to see her. And I understand when she saw my brothers, she bent down and kissed their feet. She was so overwhelmed. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was the only time they saw her. I've not seen her ever again. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father gave up the apartment. And now that I left the children's home, because I think-- no, I did not leave the children's home yet. I was at my uncle's apartment, that was also in the same 20th-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] District.

speaker: interviewee
text: District, in the 20th district. Within walking distance of-- from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your Uncle Max?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncle Max, yes. We were there, and we talked things over. And my father and my brothers, they decided that we would go illegally over the border. Oh, I forgot to tell you, my father went out with a woman called Blanca. And they were going to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lust. L-U-S-T, Lust. She was married previously, but she had no children. And she loved my father, my father loved her, and they were going steady. But she lived her own apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I liked her. I liked her. [INAUDIBLE] She was a stranger to me, but she tried to be nice to me. And later on, I got to like her very much. We became friends. It was decided that we would go over the border. Blanca was going to go to London. She had a brother, a physician, living there. She was going to go there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But shall I tell you, we went-- my father and the three children-- we went through Germany to Aachen.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was maybe in August [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of 1938. Shortly after the eviction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you remember leaving Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. How was that? What happened? Well, Vienna-- Vienna was so bad. It was so bad. The anti-Semitism, everything. Everything bad that ever happened to the Austrians was the fault of the Jews. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- I really don't understand it, but I must really say something that I really believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since young children never failed to insult to us because of-- of being Jewish, I must say it was handed down from generation to generation. How do children learn? By their parents. Why would somebody call us pig Jew, and all sorts of names, when they are six, seven years old? Anti-Semitism was bred from generation to generation.

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved Vienna. But I certainly was glad to leave it. I was completely confused. We had no home, we had nothing, everything was taken away. We had to leave. We made our way to Aachen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. Went all through Germany. I was 11, 13, and 15, the three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I remember vividly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was scary, but-- but I felt safe with my father. My father was always my-- my savior. I don't know. And my older brother, my brothers-- I felt safe with my family. But I knew that safety was only while he was with me. I knew that-- I understood the enemy around us.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop this tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good.
